inputs.story: The Bennington Kennel Club is putting together a feature article on some of their past dog show winners. Match each dog to its breed and owner, and determine the year in which it won "Best in Show". Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
inputs.constraints:
1. The dalmatian won 1 year after Fernando's canine.
2. Ginger's dog won 1 year before the irish setter.
3. Ginger's canine is the bulldog.
4. The four dogs are the dog that won in 2006, the dalmatian, the bulldog and Elsie's dog.
inputs.constants:
owners: Anita; Elsie; Fernando; Ginger.
years: 2006; 2007; 2008; 2009.
breeds: bulldog; dalmatian; irish setter; maltese.
inputs.solution:
owners	years	breeds
ginger	2008	bulldog
anita	2007	dalmatian
elsie	2009	irish setter
fernando	2006	maltese

asp_generation.prompt:
<task>
You are given a logic grid puzzle with a story, numbered clues, and categorized constants. Your task is to generate a complete ASP (Answer Set Programming) program that, when solved with Clingo, yields a unique answer set representing the puzzle's solution.

Provided below are:
- two examples: `example_1` and `example_2`,
- the problem you must solve: `problem_to_solve`,
- important rules: `rules`.
</task>

<example_1>
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
Employees: Bonita; Yvette; Tabitha.
Prices: $225; $275; $325.
Wood types: ash; poplar; sandalwood.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
employee("Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha").
price(225; 275; 325).
wood_type("ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one price P and wood type W.
{match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; P1=P2; W1=W2}=0 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), (E1,P1,W1)!=(E2,P2,W2).

% 1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".

% 2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".

% 3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".

% 4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.

#show match/3.
```
</example_1>

<example_2>
The Winter Olympics have just wrapped up in Norway. Using only the clues that follow, determine the number of gold and silver medals won by each country. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.

Constants:
Countries: Argentina; Bolivia; Grenada; Oman.
Silver medals: 2; 6; 10; 11.
Gold medals: 1; 2; 3; 4.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
country("Argentina"; "Bolivia"; "Grenada"; "Oman").
silver_medals(2; 6; 10; 11).
gold_medals(1; 2; 3; 4).

% Search space: for each country C, assign exactly one silver medal count S and gold medal count G.
{assign(C, S, G): silver_medals(S), gold_medals(G)}=1 :- country(C).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{C1=C2; S1=S2; G1=G2}=0 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), (C1,S1,G1)!=(C2,S2,G2).

% 1. The four teams are all different — Bolivia, the 3-gold team, the 6-silver team, and Argentina each refer to distinct countries.
{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).

% 2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Oman", S2=10.

% 3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
{G=2; G=1}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), C="Oman".

% 4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams — three pairwise inequalities.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C2!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C1!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".

% 5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
{C="Argentina"; S=2}=0 :- assign(C,S,G), G=4.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Argentina", S2=2.

% 6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.
{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.

#show assign/3.
```
</example_2>

Here is the problem to be solved:
<problem_to_solve>
The Bennington Kennel Club is putting together a feature article on some of their past dog show winners. Match each dog to its breed and owner, and determine the year in which it won "Best in Show". Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The dalmatian won 1 year after Fernando's canine.
2. Ginger's dog won 1 year before the irish setter.
3. Ginger's canine is the bulldog.
4. The four dogs are the dog that won in 2006, the dalmatian, the bulldog and Elsie's dog.

Constants:
owners: Anita; Elsie; Fernando; Ginger.
years: 2006; 2007; 2008; 2009.
breeds: bulldog; dalmatian; irish setter; maltese.
</problem_to_solve>

Here are some important rules to follow when generating the ASP code:
<rules>
1. Constant declarations — "category(const1; const2; ...)."
   - Category names: lowercase with underscores only (e.g., wood_type, gold_medals).
   - All constants in the same category must use the same format — all integers or all quoted strings.
   - Prefer integers: strip special characters ($225 → 225), strip units while keeping the number ("3 days" → 3, "2 months" → 2), and convert verbal or ordinal numerals (once → 1, twice → 2, third → 3, September → 9).
   - Use double-quoted strings when no integer representation is possible (e.g., "Bonita", "9:30AM").

2. Predicates
   - Use the minimum number of predicates needed to relate all categories.
   - Every category must appear in at least one predicate argument; all arguments must be distinct variables.
   - Choose a name that reflects the relationship (e.g., match, assign, schedule, launch).

3. Search space — one choice rule per predicate, anchoring on one category in the body and ranging over the rest inside the braces:
     {pred(V1, V2, ...): cat2(V2), cat3(V3), ...}=1 :- cat1(V1).

4. Uniqueness — no value in any non-anchor category appears more than once:
     {V1=V2; W1=W2; ...}=0 :- pred(V1,W1,...), pred(V2,W2,...), (V1,W1,...)!=(V2,W2,...).

5. Constraint rules — encode each clue using one or more rules of these forms, using only defined constants and predicates:
     <C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.      % if all Li hold, at least one Ci must hold
     {<C1>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.  % if all Li hold, exactly k of Ci hold
     :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.                        % this combination is forbidden

   Common patterns:
   - "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" → three rules: A≠B, A is C or D, B is C or D.
   - "A, B and C are all different" → three pairwise inequality rules (A≠B, A≠C, B≠C).
   - "Neither A nor B is X" → {A_cond; B_cond}=0 :- ... X ... .

6. Show directives — one "#show pred/arity." per predicate.
</rules>

ASP Program:
asp_generation.thinking:
Here's a thinking process:

1.  **Analyze the Problem:**
   - **Goal:** Match each dog to its breed and owner, and determine the year it won "Best in Show".
   - **Categories:**
     - Owners: Anita, Elsie, Fernando, Ginger
     - Years: 2006, 2007, 2008, 2009
     - Breeds: bulldog, dalmatian, irish setter, maltese
   - **Clues:**
     1. The dalmatian won 1 year after Fernando's canine.
     2. Ginger's dog won 1 year before the irish setter.
     3. Ginger's canine is the bulldog.
     4. The four dogs are the dog that won in 2006, the dalmatian, the bulldog and Elsie's dog.
   - **Constraints:** Each owner gets exactly one breed and one year. No repeats within a category.

2.  **Map to ASP Structure:**
   - **Constants:**
     - `owner("Anita"; "Elsie"; "Fernando"; "Ginger").`
     - `year(2006; 2007; 2008; 2009).`
     - `breed("bulldog"; "dalmatian"; "irish setter"; "maltese").`
   - **Predicate:** `win(Owner, Year, Breed)` or `match(Owner, Year, Breed)`. Let's use `win` or `dog_info`. I'll stick to `match` or `win` as in examples. The examples use `match` or `assign`. I'll use `win(O, Y, B)`.
   - **Search Space:**
     `{win(O, Y, B): year(Y), breed(B)}=1 :- owner(O).`
   - **Uniqueness:**
     `{O1=O2; Y1=Y2; B1=B2}=0 :- win(O1,Y1,B1), win(O2,Y2,B2), (O1,Y1,B1)!=(O2,Y2,B2).`
   - **Clue Encoding:**
     - Clue 1: "The dalmatian won 1 year after Fernando's canine."
       - Let `win(O1, Y1, B1)` be Fernando's dog: `O1="Fernando"`. Year = `Y1`.
       - Let `win(O2, Y2, B2)` be the dalmatian: `B2="dalmatian"`.
       - Relationship: `Y2 = Y1 + 1`.
       - Rule: `Y2 = Y1 + 1 :- win(O1,Y1,B1), win(O2,Y2,B2), O1="Fernando", B2="dalmatian".`
     - Clue 2: "Ginger's dog won 1 year before the irish setter."
       - Let `win(O1, Y1, B1)` be Ginger's dog: `O1="Ginger"`. Year = `Y1`.
       - Let `win(O2, Y2, B2)` be the irish setter: `B2="irish setter"`.
       - Relationship: `Y1 = Y2 - 1` (or `Y2 = Y1 + 1`).
       - Rule: `Y2 = Y1 + 1 :- win(O1,Y1,B1), win(O2,Y2,B2), O1="Ginger", B2="irish setter".`
     - Clue 3: "Ginger's canine is the bulldog."
       - Direct assignment: `B="bulldog" :- win(O,Breed,Y), O="Ginger".` (Note: order of args in my predicate is `win(Owner, Year, Breed)`, so I need to be careful with variable names).
       - Let's define predicate as `win(O, Y, B)`.
       - Rule: `B="bulldog" :- win(O,Y,B), O="Ginger".`
     - Clue 4: "The four dogs are the dog that won in 2006, the dalmatian, the bulldog and Elsie's dog."
       - This implies these four refer to four distinct entities. So:
         - Dog with Year=2006
         - Dog with Breed="dalmatian"
         - Dog with Breed="bulldog"
         - Dog with Owner="Elsie"
       - All four must be different.
       - We need pairwise inequality constraints among these four descriptions.
       - Let's denote:
         - `win(O_a, Y_a, B_a)` where `Y_a=2006`
         - `win(O_b, Y_b, B_b)` where `B_b="dalmatian"`
         - `win(O_c, Y_c, B_c)` where `B_c="bulldog"`
         - `win(O_d, Y_d, B_d)` where `O_d="Elsie"`
       - Constraints:
         - `O_a!=O_d :- win(O_a,Y_a,B_a), win(O_d,Y_d,B_d), Y_a=2006, O_d="Elsie".` (Year 2006 != Elsie)
         - `O_a!=O_b :- win(O_a,Y_a,B_a), win(O_b,Y_b,B_b), Y_a=2006, B_b="dalmatian".` (Year 2006 != Dalmatian)
         - `O_a!=O_c :- win(O_a,Y_a,B_a), win(O_c,Y_c,B_c), Y_a=2006, B_c="bulldog".` (Year 2006 != Bulldog)
         - `O_b!=O_d :- win(O_b,Y_b,B_b), win(O_d,Y_d,B_d), B_b="dalmatian", O_d="Elsie".` (Dalmatian != Elsie)
         - `O_b!=O_c :- win(O_b,Y_b,B_b), win(O_c,Y_c,B_c), B_b="dalmatian", B_c="bulldog".` (Dalmatian != Bulldog)
         - `O_c!=O_d :- win(O_c,Y_c,B_c), win(O_d,Y_d,B_d), B_c="bulldog", O_d="Elsie".` (Bulldog != Elsie)
       - Wait, the clue says "The four dogs are...". This is a standard grid puzzle clue meaning these four descriptions point to four distinct items. So pairwise inequality is correct.
       - Let's verify if we can simplify or if the example format prefers a specific way. Example 2 clue 1: `{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).` This is for "The four teams are...". Actually, that example uses a cardinality constraint `=1` to say exactly one of those conditions matches each team. But that's for a 4x4 grid where each row has exactly one match. Here, we have owners as the anchor. So for each owner, exactly one of those four descriptions applies? Wait, the clue says "The four dogs are the dog that won in 2006, the dalmatian, the bulldog and Elsie's dog." This means the set of all dogs corresponds exactly to these four descriptions. Since there are 4 dogs total, each description must correspond to a unique dog. So pairwise inequality is the standard way, or we can use the `{...}=1` pattern if we anchor on the dog itself, but we anchor on owners.
       - Let's stick to pairwise inequalities for clarity, as it's robust.
       - Actually, the `{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).` in Example 2 means for each country, exactly one of those properties holds. That works because there are exactly 4 countries and 4 properties. Here, we have 4 owners and 4 properties (Year=2006, Breed="dalmatian", Breed="bulldog", Owner="Elsie"). So for each owner, exactly one of these properties holds. That's equivalent to pairwise inequality + covering. But pairwise inequality is safer and standard for "The four X are A, B, C, D".
       - I will use pairwise inequalities. It's explicit and matches the "A, B and C are all different" pattern in the rules.
       - Let's list them carefully:
         - `Y1!=Y2 :- win(O1,Y1,B1), win(O2,Y2,B2), Y1=2006, Y2=2006, (O1,Y1,B1)!=(O2,Y2,B2).` (Actually, if they are the same dog, it's the same tuple. So just `O1!=O2 :- win(O1,Y1,B1), win(O2,Y2,B2), Y1=2006, Y2=2006, (O1,Y1,B1)!=(O2,Y2,B2).` But we can just say `O1!=O2 :- win(O1,Y1,B1), win(O2,Y2,B2), Y1=2006, O2="Elsie", (O1,Y1,B1)!=(O2,Y2,B2).` etc.)
       - Let's simplify using the pattern from Example 2 Clue 4:
         `C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".`
         I'll follow a similar explicit style.
         Actually, the `{...}=1` pattern is often cleaner for "The four X are A, B, C, D". Let's try:
         `{Y=2006; B="dalmatian"; B="bulldog"; O="Elsie"}=1 :- win(O,Y,B).`
         This says for each dog, exactly one of these applies. Since there are 4 dogs and 4 descriptions, this forces a bijection. This is very elegant and matches Example 2's style. I'll use this.
       - Wait, check Example 2 Clue 1: `{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).`
         Yes, this is exactly the pattern. I will use it.

3.  **Draft ASP Code:**
   ```asp
   % Constants
   owner("Anita"; "Elsie"; "Fernando"; "Ginger").
   year(2006; 2007; 2008; 2009).
   breed("bulldog"; "dalmatian"; "irish setter"; "maltese").

   % Search space
   {win(O, Y, B): year(Y), breed(B)}=1 :- owner(O).

   % Uniqueness
   {O1=O2; Y1=Y2; B1=B2}=0 :- win(O1,Y1,B1), win(O2,Y2,B2), (O1,Y1,B1)!=(O2,Y2,B2).

   % Clue 1: The dalmatian won 1 year after Fernando's canine.
   Y2 = Y1 + 1 :- win(O1,Y1,B1), win(O2,Y2,B2), O1="Fernando", B2="dalmatian".

   % Clue 2: Ginger's dog won 1 year before the irish setter.
   Y2 = Y1 + 1 :- win(O1,Y1,B1), win(O2,Y2,B2), O1="Ginger", B2="irish setter".

   % Clue 3: Ginger's canine is the bulldog.
   B="bulldog" :- win(O,Y,B), O="Ginger".

   % Clue 4: The four dogs are the dog that won in 2006, the dalmatian, the bulldog and Elsie's dog.
   {Y=2006; B="dalmatian"; B="bulldog"; O="Elsie"}=1 :- win(O,Y,B).

   #show win/3.
   ```

4.  **Verify & Refine:**
   - Check constants format: `owner`, `year`, `breed`. All lowercase with underscores. Constants are strings or ints. `year` uses ints. `owner` and `breed` use strings. Matches rules.
   - Search space: `{win(O, Y, B): year(Y), breed(B)}=1 :- owner(O).` Matches rule 3.
   - Uniqueness: `{O1=O2; Y1=Y2; B1=B2}=0 :- win(O1,Y1,B1), win(O2,Y2,B2), (O1,Y1,B1)!=(O2,Y2,B2).` Matches rule 4.
   - Clue 1: `Y2 = Y1 + 1 :- win(O1,Y1,B1), win(O2,Y2,B2), O1="Fernando", B2="dalmatian".` Correct. "1 year after" means Dalmatian year = Fernando year + 1.
   - Clue 2: `Y2 = Y1 + 1 :- win(O1,Y1,B1), win(O2,Y2,B2), O1="Ginger", B2="irish setter".` Correct. "1 year before" means Ginger year = Irish Setter year - 1, so Irish Setter year = Ginger year + 1.
   - Clue 3: `B="bulldog" :- win(O,Y,B), O="Ginger".` Correct.
   - Clue 4: `{Y=2006; B="dalmatian"; B="bulldog"; O="Elsie"}=1 :- win(O,Y,B).` Correct. Matches the pattern for "The four dogs are...".
   - Check for potential conflicts or ambiguity:
     - From Clue 3: Ginger = bulldog.
     - From Clue 4: Since Ginger = bulldog, the description "bulldog" applies to Ginger. So Ginger cannot be "Elsie" (obviously), cannot be "dalmatian", cannot be "2006".
     - From Clue 2: Ginger's dog (bulldog) won 1 year before irish setter. So Year(bulldog) = Year(irish setter) - 1.
     - From Clue 1: Dalmatian won 1 year after Fernando's canine. So Year(dalmatian) = Year(Fernando) + 1.
     - Years are 2006, 2007, 2008, 2009.
     - Let's mentally solve to ensure uniqueness:
       - Ginger = bulldog.
       - Clue 4 says each dog matches exactly one of {Y=2006, B=dalmatian, B=bulldog, O=Elsie}.
       - Since Ginger=bulldog, Ginger is not Y=2006, not B=dalmatian, not O=Elsie.
       - So Y=2006 must be one of: Anita, Fernando, Elsie. But Elsie is O=Elsie, so Y=2006 is either Anita or Fernando.
       - B=dalmatian must be one of: Anita, Elsie, Fernando. (Ginger is bulldog)
       - O=Elsie must be one of: Anita, Elsie, Fernando. (Ginger is bulldog)
       - Let's assign owners to years/breeds.
       - Years: 2006, 2007, 2008, 2009.
       - Clue 2: Ginger(bulldog) year = Irish setter year - 1.
       - Clue 1: Dalmatian year = Fernando year + 1.
       - Since years are consecutive integers, possible pairs for (Ginger, Irish setter) are (2006,2007), (2007,2008), (2008,2009).
       - Possible pairs for (Fernando, Dalmatian) are (2006,2007), (2007,2008), (2008,2009).
       - They can't overlap on owners unless it's the same person, but Ginger != Fernando.
       - If Ginger=2006, Irish setter=2007.
       - If Fernando=2007, Dalmatian=2008. Then Ginger(2006) != Fernando(2007). Irish setter(2007) != Dalmatian(2008). This works.
         - Owners: Ginger=2006(bulldog), Fernando=2007(?), Irish setter=2007(Owner?), Dalmatian=2008(Owner?).
         - Wait, Irish setter is a breed. Dalmatian is a breed.
         - Let's list:
           - Ginger: 2006, bulldog
           - Fernando: 2007, ?
           - Irish setter owner: 2007, irish setter
           - Dalmatian owner: 2008, dalmatian
           - Remaining owner: Anita or Elsie. Remaining year: 2009. Remaining breed: maltese.
           - Clue 4: {Y=2006; B=dalmatian; B=bulldog; O=Elsie}=1.
           - We have:
             - 2006: Ginger, bulldog. (Matches B=bulldog)
             - 2007: Fernando, irish setter. (Matches O=Fernando, Y=2007, B=irish setter) -> Wait, does it match any of the 4 conditions? No. But Clue 4 says *each* of the 4 dogs matches *exactly one* of the 4 conditions. So the 2007 dog must match one of them. But it matches none? Contradiction!
           - Let's re-evaluate Clue 4 carefully.
           - "{Y=2006; B="dalmatian"; B="bulldog"; O="Elsie"}=1 :- win(O,Y,B)."
           - This means for every dog, exactly one of these is true.
           - If Fernando's dog is in 2007, it doesn't match Y=2006. If his breed is not dalmatian/bulldog, and he's not Elsie, then it matches 0 conditions. That violates the constraint.
           - So Fernando's dog MUST match one of the conditions. Since he's not Elsie, and his dog isn't bulldog or dalmatian (from our hypothetical), it fails.
           - Let's solve properly.
           - We have 4 dogs. Each must satisfy exactly one of: Y=2006, B=dalmatian, B=bulldog, O=Elsie.
           - We know Ginger = bulldog. So Ginger satisfies B=bulldog.
           - The dalmatian satisfies B=dalmatian.
           - Elsie satisfies O=Elsie.
           - The 2006 winner satisfies Y=2006.
           - These four must be distinct dogs. So:
             - Ginger (bulldog) != Dalmatian owner
             - Ginger (bulldog) != Elsie
             - Ginger (bulldog) != 2006 winner
             - Dalmatian owner != Elsie
             - Dalmatian owner != 2006 winner
             - Elsie != 2006 winner
           - So:
             - Ginger is not 2006 winner.
             - Dalmatian owner is not 2006 winner.
             - Elsie is not 2006 winner.
             - Therefore, Fernando must be the 2006 winner! (Since owners are Anita, Elsie, Fernando, Ginger. Ginger!=2006, Dalmatian owner!=2006, Elsie!=2006. Wait, Dalmatian owner could be Anita or Fernando. If Dalmatian owner is Fernando, then Fernando is 2006 winner AND Dalmatian owner. But they must be distinct. So Fernando cannot be Dalmatian owner. Thus, 2006 winner must be Fernando.)
             - So Fernando = 2006.
           - Check Clue 1: Dalmatian won 1 year after Fernando's canine. Fernando=2006, so Dalmatian=2007.
           - Check Clue 2: Ginger's dog won 1 year before irish setter. Ginger != 2006. Ginger's year could be 2007, 2008, 2009.
             - If Ginger=2007, Irish setter=2008.
             - If Ginger=2008, Irish setter=2009.
             - We know Dalmatian=2007. So Ginger != 2007 (since Ginger=bulldog).
             - So Ginger must be 2008 or 2009.
             - If Ginger=2009, Irish setter=2010 (invalid). So Ginger=2008.
             - Thus Ginger=2008 (bulldog). Irish setter=2009.
             - Remaining year for Elsie/Anita: 2007. But Dalmatian=2007. So Elsie or Anita is Dalmatian owner at 2007.
             - We have owners: Fernando(2006), Ginger(2008), Dalmatian owner(2007), Irish setter owner(2009).
             - Irish setter owner is at 2009.
             - Dalmatian owner is at 2007.
             - Remaining owners: Anita, Elsie.
             - Remaining years assigned: Fernando(2006), Dalmatian(2007), Ginger(2008), Irish setter(2009).

owner("Anita"; "Elsie"; "Fernando"; "Ginger").
year(2006; 2007; 2008; 2009).
breed("bulldog"; "dalmatian"; "irish setter"; "maltese").

% Search space: for each owner O, assign exactly one year Y and breed B.
{match(O, Y, B): year(Y), breed(B)}=1 :- owner(O).

{O1=O2; Y1=Y2; B1=B2}=0 :- match(O1,Y1,B1), match(O2,Y2,B2), (O1,Y1,B1)!=(O2,Y2,B2).

% 2. Ginger's dog won 1 year before the irish setter.
